turns.content: what's the difference between using the guard macro for async validation versus writing a custom validator trait manually?

The main distinction lies in how you handle asynchronous operations and define rules. The garde crate provides an async-aware derive macro that lets you attach validators to structs directly, whereas manual traits require you to implement the logic yourself and potentially use a separate validation context.

I thought I could just call the guard method on any struct without modifying it first.

I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept validator for my hobby project using the garde crate, but I keep getting errors about missing traits. I thought deriving Serialize on my struct would be enough since serde_json handles that.

That is a common misconception. The garde crate does not rely on serde's Serialize trait for its core validation logic, so you do not need to implement or derive it unless you are specifically using the async adapter which requires custom types that might need serialization later. For standard synchronous validation with the rules DSL, just define your struct normally.

Okay, I defined a User struct and created some simple rules like email format checks using the built-in validators. But when I try to run the async version of the validator with the new adapter, it fails because my custom type is not recognized.

The issue lies in how you are defining your custom types for the async adapter. When using the garde crate's async capabilities, any struct you want to validate must implement the specific trait required by that adapter, which is different from standard Serialize implementations.

I tried implementing that trait manually but I am getting a compilation error saying the field types are incompatible with what the rule system expects. Is there a way to map my custom fields directly without complex conversions?

You do not need complex mappings if you structure your data correctly from the start. The best approach is to define your validation rules DSL to match exactly the shape of your struct, ensuring that every field in your type corresponds directly to a rule or an optional validator in your configuration.

I managed to get the basic async flow working now. However, I am wondering if there is a way to extend the rules DSL with my own custom validators that do something very specific like checking for historical data consistency without relying on external crates.

Yes, you can easily create your own validators by implementing the trait provided by the crate. This allows you to write custom logic inline within your validation pipeline, giving you full control over complex edge cases like your historical data requirement without needing to pull in additional dependencies.

That sounds perfect for my use case. One last thing though, I noticed the documentation mentions something about combining synchronous and asynchronous flows. Can we actually chain both of them together seamlessly?

You can absolutely combine them by using the appropriate adapters for each context. If you need to run a quick check synchronously before an expensive async validation, you simply call the synchronous validator first and then pass its result into the async adapter's input type.

It's like when I tried to debug a failing CI/CD pipeline earlier and realized my validation logic was just throwing errors instead of returning structured data. I'm building a new form validator in Rust and thought maybe the garde crate could help me avoid those messy error strings.

That's a great comparison because handling unstructured error messages is exactly why you want to use a proper validation crate like garde. The core idea with garde is that it gives you a rules DSL where you define constraints on fields, and then the system automatically generates the right error types for your application instead of manual string concatenation.

I've been using serde_json::Value to hold form data but I'm struggling with how to actually apply those rules. Is there a derive macro that automatically wires up the validation logic so I don't have to manually write loops checking every field?

Yes, you can use the derive macros on your structs along with guards to define constraints like length limits or regex patterns. For example, if you create a struct that derives From<serde_json::Value>, garde will automatically validate it against those rules and return a Result type instead of panicking.
I tried writing a custom validator function but I keep getting compiler errors about lifetime elision. Does garde support async validation or do I have to stick with synchronous checks?

You can definitely use async validation by combining garde with tokio and the futures library, though you need to be careful about how you structure your async blocks. The main thing is that garde itself provides synchronous rules DSL but it integrates well with async runtimes when you wrap its output in an Async trait implementation.
I think I understand the basic flow now, but what happens if my custom validator throws a panic instead of returning an error? Will the CI/CD pipeline just fail silently?

That's actually exactly the common mistake beginners make when working with validation crates. If your custom validator panics, it will crash the entire runtime and you'll get no structured error logs to debug what went wrong.
So how do I prevent that panic from happening? Should I wrap my logic in a try block or use something else?

You should avoid using panic entirely and instead return an error using the Result type. When you write your custom validator, make sure it returns Ok(some_value) on success or Err(error_message) on failure so garde can handle the error gracefully.
I'm still confused about how to integrate this into my existing CI/CD pipeline. Does garde work with cargo test or do I need a separate validation step?

You can integrate garde directly into your cargo test suite by running the validation checks as part of your test harness. The crate will automatically catch invalid inputs during your tests and generate detailed error reports that you can use to improve your pipeline.
One more thing I'm wondering about is how does garde handle nested structures? Like if I have a user object with an address field, will it validate the address recursively?

Yes, garde handles recursive validation automatically when you define rules on nested structs. If your User struct contains an Address sub-struct and you apply a rule to Address, the entire validation chain will check both levels without extra code.
Wait, so I don't have to manually unwrap values or create intermediate variables? That sounds like it would save me tons of boilerplate code compared to what I'm doing now.
Exactly. The derive macros and rules DSL handle all the recursive unwrapping for you, which means your code stays clean and focused on business logic rather than validation plumbing.
